workMixDesign.sourceDerivedSummary: For this mid-level game designer profile, the strongest signals are digital tool and artifact work, cognitive design decisions, written specs, and frequent cross-functional communication. O*NET, YouTube transcripts, and alternative public industry sources describe designers/planners creating design documents, flowcharts, proposals, test plans, level blockouts, spreadsheets, playtest loops, prototypes, acceptance criteria, and meeting-heavy coordination with specialists. Physical device/controller use appears, but it is supporting context rather than the core work.

sourceEvidence: A Japanese game planner verifies bugs on physical test phones and uses test devices when simulators are not enough.
A Street Fighter planner checks gameplay using two controllers.
A level designer builds and plays rough blockouts, but the core manipulation is digital/editor work.
physicalProceduralTool.simulatorImplication: Do not create a physical playground; represent the hands-on part through concrete digital design notes and playtest-based priority ranking.

sourceEvidence: O*NET lists design documentation, mock-up screenshots, flowcharts, test plans, and QA specifications.
Practitioner sources mention documents, presentations, spreadsheets, editor work, blockouts, and repeated playtesting.
Alternative sources from The Level Design Book, Riot Games, GitBook, and Game Developer emphasize blockouts, prototypes, designer tools, GDDs, and shared design artifacts.
digitalToolArtifactWork.simulatorImplication: Most active scenes use Notion/doc, playtest note capture, Slack, voice review, and email work surfaces.

sourceEvidence: O*NET emphasizes balancing gameplay, devising challenges, solving problems, and judging quality.
Designer sources emphasize finding fun inside technical, budget, IP, team, and time constraints.
Alternative sources such as The Door Problem and Riot prototype articles frame game design as player-experience problem solving under cross-discipline and scope constraints.
cognitiveAnalysisDecision.simulatorImplication: The player must frame the player problem, define observable playtest signals, prioritize evidence, and defend tradeoffs.

sourceEvidence: O*NET lists formal design documentation, production schedules, prototype goals, communication plans, and test specifications.
A designer describes spending blocks of time on design projects, reviewing content, and providing feedback between meetings.
GitBook and Game Developer public articles describe game design documents as a source of truth and communication artifact for teams.
writtenDocumentationArtifact.simulatorImplication: Require typed hook notes, spec updates, meeting prep, and a live engineering handoff.

sourceEvidence: O*NET lists daily email, face-to-face discussion, and work with teams as important context.
Day-in-the-life videos show planners meeting with level, effects, costume, battle, and project teams.
Riot's agile creative development article emphasizes cross-functional teams, standups, playtest reviews, QA, and acceptance criteria.
spokenInterpersonalCommunication.simulatorImplication: Include a realistic in-person engineering handoff where the student clarifies buildable rules and edge cases.

sourceEvidence: A professional designer describes multiple meetings throughout the day and design work in blocks between meetings.
Game planner sources show movement between meetings, desk work, team play sessions, and bug verification.
Riot's development article reinforces the rhythm of playtests, reviews, and feedback loops inside creative development.
passiveMonitoringWaitingContextSwitching.simulatorImplication: Use briefings and Slack interruptions to create context switching, without making passive reading dominate the simulator.

workMixDesign.simulatorPlan: The simulator is a single milestone day on a family-friendly AAA console project. The player reads a build workspace, drafts one focused mechanic teaching note, turns it into a playtest watchlist, observes a small playtest session, ranks the fix priorities from observed evidence, writes an implementation spec email, prepares for and speaks in an in-person engineering handoff, then reaches the explicit assessment gate.
workMixDesign.activeSceneMixRationale: Active scenes are mostly digital design and documentation, with one Slack decision and one spoken review. This matches the source-derived profile: game design is concrete tool/artifact work, playtest interpretation, written specs, and cross-functional communication rather than generic creativity or physical labor.

storyline.gameTitle: Wind Bud Milestone: A Game Designer Day

intro.title: Welcome to Starling Grove Studios
intro.content: You are the mid-level Game Designer on Sproutbound, a fictional family-friendly AAA console adventure. Today you need to make the new Wind Bud traversal mechanic easier to learn without blowing up the milestone scope.
intro.imageBrief: A bright, polished AAA game studio design area with warm wood desks, concept art of cute forest guardians on the walls, a console controller, design notebooks, and a large screen showing a whimsical green platforming level. No real company logos.

morning_briefing.title: Morning Build Workspace
morning_briefing.content: Your job today: make the first Wind Bud room easier to learn without losing the fun.

subSteps.content: The team has two playable hours before the next internal playtest. Your first task is to convert the messy player problem into one teachable design note.

slackMessages.content: Morning. The Wind Bud toy is charming, but first-time players are not reading it. Give me one clear teaching note before we touch the blockout.
The current room has a safe platform, one bud, a coin trail, and a late tutorial hint. It still feels like players look everywhere except the gust.

subSteps.content: Owen's quick notes are rough but useful. Treat them as evidence, not as a script.

metrics.metric: Players who noticed first Wind Bud without hint
Average retries before first successful glide
Players who reached the second platform

quotes.speaker: Playtester A
quotes.text: I thought the flower was background until the tutorial hint popped up.

quotes.speaker: Playtester C
quotes.text: The gust starts behind me, so I jump away from the thing I need.

inputArtifacts.label: Source workspace
inputArtifacts.summary: Real workplace source apps and files opened by the player.

deskWorkDesign.studentAction: Open the source apps and files before choosing the first work move.
continuity: Source facts reappear in later app tabs, work surfaces, and conversation prep.
deskWorkDesign.authenticityCheck: The player starts from realistic workplace messages, apps, folders, and dashboards rather than a generic briefing bundle.

morning_briefing.imageBrief: First-person view at a bright AAA game studio desk during the morning build review. A cream laptop and notebook are open, a controller sits nearby, and a wall monitor in the background shows a whimsical forest platforming level with a small wind-flower mechanic. Keep all screen text generic or unreadable; the actual brief, Slack messages, metrics, and quotes are rendered by the simulator UI.

files.name: Build 0.43 tracker.doc

sections.heading: Build tracker
sections.body: Family-friendly console prototype. Today focus: the first Wind Bud room. Rule: strike the Wind Bud to open a short-lived gust cone; enter the gust from the safe side to lift or glide across a small gap. Current room: safe start platform, one Wind Bud, second platform, optional coin trail, and a tutorial hint that triggers too late. Known risk: players miss the bud, approach the gust from the wrong side, or chase coins before learning the core movement.

files.lookFor: Use this tracker to understand the intended Wind Bud rule, the room goal, and current design risks before changing anything.

files.name: Morning playtest notes.txt

sections.heading: Playtest notes
sections.body: Players like the Wind Bud once they use it, but several think the flower is background. Two players jump directly across the gap and fall repeatedly. One player strikes the bud, then runs away from the gust because it starts behind them. The coin trail pulls attention away before the player understands the Wind Bud rule.

files.lookFor: Use these notes to find where players misunderstand Wind Bud and which confusion repeats across playtests.

messages.body: Open the build tracker, playtest notes, and telemetry before touching the level. I need a systems read, not random polish.
messages.lookFor: Use this Slack message to understand the lead’s priority, the playtest concern, and which source files to open first.

apps.label: Dashboard
apps.title: Build telemetry

rows.metric: Optional coin route before core lesson

apps.lookFor: Use these rows to connect player confusion with measurable room behavior before proposing a fix.

mechanic_hook_sheet.title: Wind Bud Teaching Note
mechanic_hook_sheet.content: Use the mini level map to teach the Wind Bud like a designer. The map is static right now: no attention effect, no motion, no success feedback has been added yet. Pick one part of the room you want to improve, then write what cue/effect you would add and what feedback would prove the rule worked.
mechanic_hook_sheet.prompt: Make one playable design note. Choose one level area, then answer only what cue/effect you would add and what success feedback would prove the rule. The five map areas are choices, not a checklist; unused areas can stay plain.

fields.label: Level area
fields.placeholder: Choose the level area this note focuses on.

fields.label: Cue/effect you add
fields.placeholder: Think about what should catch the player's eye, and what should separate it from the background.

fields.label: Success feedback you add
fields.placeholder: Think about what should change after success so the player can tell the rule worked.

visualBoard.title: Wind Bud tutorial slice
visualBoard.subtitle: Click one place you want to improve. The current image is static, so your note is the cue/effect and feedback you would add.
visualBoard.helperText: You are not describing finished UI. You are choosing where to add a cue, motion, sound, layout change, or success feedback. The five areas are possible targets, not required fields; unused areas can stay plain.
visualBoard.imageAlt: A simple side-view tutorial room with a safe start platform, Wind Bud, gust cone, landing platform, and optional coins.

hotspots.label: Safe start platform
hotspots.description: Where the player can stop, look, and experiment safely.

hotspots.label: Wind Bud
hotspots.description: The object the player must notice and strike.

hotspots.label: Gust cone
hotspots.description: The wind path that shows where to enter and travel.

hotspots.label: Landing platform
hotspots.description: The safe proof that the lift worked.

hotspots.label: Optional coin trail
hotspots.description: A reward path that should not distract before the lesson.

beats.label: Teaching note
beats.helper: Think about what should draw the player's eye first, then what should change after success so the player knows the Wind Bud rule worked.

inputArtifacts.label: Feature Brief - Wind Bud Tutorial
inputArtifacts.summary: Mechanic rule, current room layout, player confusion, and constraints.

inputArtifacts.label: Clickable Wind Bud level slice
inputArtifacts.summary: A side-view map of the safe start, Wind Bud, gust cone, landing platform, and optional coin trail.

deskWorkDesign.studentAction: Pick one useful map area, then write the cue/effect and success feedback the designer would add.

outputArtifact.label: Wind Bud teaching note
outputArtifact.format: visual annotation plus structured design note

continuity: The visual teaching note informs the playtest watchlist and implementation handoff.
deskWorkDesign.authenticityCheck: A designer annotates the playable slice so the team can see how the mechanic is taught in the room.

playtest_watchlist.title: Wind Bud Playtest Watchlist
playtest_watchlist.content: Maya wants the 4 PM playtest to check whether your Teaching Note actually helps first-time players. Do not pitch another feature here. Turn your cue/effect idea into a short observer watchlist: what to watch, what counts as success, what counts as confusion, and what not to over-read.
playtest_watchlist.prompt: Write one watchlist item for the next playtest. Keep it observable: a tester should be able to mark it while watching the player, without asking the player to explain the design.

previousResponseReference.title: Your Wind Bud Teaching Note
previousResponseReference.emptyText: Submit the Teaching Note first to see it here.

fields.label: Behavior to watch
fields.placeholder: What should the player do on their own if the cue is working?

fields.label: Success signal
fields.placeholder: What visible action proves the Wind Bud rule made sense?

fields.label: Confusion signal
fields.placeholder: What visible action shows the player is still misreading the room?

fields.label: Do not over-read
fields.placeholder: What comment or behavior might sound positive but should not count as proof?

inputArtifacts.label: Wind Bud Teaching Note
inputArtifacts.whereVisible: previous structured entry and section briefing drawer
inputArtifacts.summary: The player's proposed cue/effect and success feedback.

inputArtifacts.label: 4 PM playtest goal
inputArtifacts.summary: Observe whether first-time players read the Wind Bud without being told.

deskWorkDesign.studentAction: Turn the teaching note into observable playtest signals.
continuity: The Run the Playtest scene asks the player to observe those same signals in tester behavior.
deskWorkDesign.authenticityCheck: Designers often translate design intent into observable playtest questions before judging whether a change worked.

spec_update.title: Implementation Spec Update
spec_update.content: Priya and Leo need a compact spec update they can act on, and Owen and Emi need clear QA and scope notes. Write it as an email so the implementation decision can move through the team.
spec_update.prompt: Draft a 90-220 word email. Include: player problem, proposed change, exact expected behavior, acceptance criteria, risk or open question, and QA check.
spec_update.windowTitle: New Message - Wind Bud Spec Update

emailHeaders.to: Priya Raman <priya@starlinggrove.studio>; Leo Navarro <leo@starlinggrove.studio>; Owen Brooks <owen@starlinggrove.studio>; Emi Tanaka <emi@starlinggrove.studio>
emailHeaders.subject: Wind Bud clarity patch - spec update

appTabs.label: Playtest Evidence
appTabs.content: Run the Playtest notes: Tester A only noticed the Wind Bud after the tutorial hint. Tester B struck the bud, then ran away because the gust spawned behind them. Tester C reached the landing, but the reward ledge question came after success. Two testers looked toward coins before proving they understood the core rule.

appTabs.label: Watchlist Focus
appTabs.content: Use your Wind Bud Playtest Watchlist, observation log, and fix-priority ranking. The spec should turn observed behavior into a buildable change and testable QA criteria, not just describe how the room feels.

appTabs.label: Team Needs
appTabs.content: Priya needs clear behavior and edge cases. Leo needs the intended player path. Owen needs testable acceptance criteria. Emi needs risk and owner clarity.

inputArtifacts.label: Playtest Evidence
inputArtifacts.summary: Observed tester behavior and issue notes.

inputArtifacts.label: Watchlist Focus
inputArtifacts.summary: Reminder to connect the watchlist signal to buildable behavior and QA criteria.

inputArtifacts.label: Team Needs
inputArtifacts.summary: What engineering, level design, QA, and production need from the spec.

deskWorkDesign.studentAction: Write an implementation-ready spec update email.
continuity: The spec is used as context for engineering handoff prep and the live handoff.
deskWorkDesign.authenticityCheck: Design spec emails must translate player evidence into buildable behavior and testable criteria for the whole implementation group.

workSurface.title: New Message - Wind Bud Spec Update

design_review_prep.title: Prepare the Engineering Handoff
design_review_prep.content: Priya read your spec email and priority ranking. Prepare for the implementation gaps she is most likely to ask about before she touches gameplay code.
design_review_prep.prompt: Optional: jot a prep note up to 150 words. If you write one, focus on whichever implementation gaps your specific patch still needs: player-facing behavior, edge cases, reset or tuning details, ownership boundaries, or QA checks.
design_review_prep.placeholder:
Missing implementation detail:
Likely Priya question:
Answer / boundary:
QA or tuning note:

appTabs.label: Handoff Agenda
appTabs.content: Priya's agenda is driven by your email and priority ranking. She will look for whatever is still underspecified: the player-facing behavior the patch must create, edge cases, reset or tuning details, ownership boundaries, and QA-verifiable acceptance criteria. Do not answer a fixed checklist; prepare the gaps your email leaves open.

inputArtifacts.label: Handoff Agenda
inputArtifacts.summary: Implementation gaps Priya may need clarified.

inputArtifacts.label: Your Priority Ranking
inputArtifacts.summary: The player's own fix-priority ranking from Run the Playtest.

deskWorkDesign.studentAction: Optionally jot a concise prep note for a live engineering conversation.
continuity: If written, the prep note appears inside the in-person engineering conversation.
deskWorkDesign.authenticityCheck: Designers often jot quick implementation-facing reminders before handing work to engineering.

design_review.title: Engineering Handoff with Priya
design_review.content: You meet Priya in the huddle room beside the playtest lab. She is ready to implement the direction from your email, but she needs any remaining gameplay rules and edge cases clarified before coding.
design_review.playerGoal: Clarify the implementation gaps in your spec update so Priya can build the patch without guessing what is design intent, what is tunable, and what acceptance criteria should prove the change works.
design_review.endpoint: Priya should close the conversation once she has clear answers to the most important implementation gaps raised by the student's email and priority ranking.
design_review.successCriteria: A strong handoff separates player-facing intent from implementation detail, names relevant edge cases or tuning rules, gives QA-verifiable criteria, and keeps the scope small enough to build.
design_review.goalPrompt: Act as Priya Raman, Gameplay Engineer, in an in-person engineering handoff. Base your questions on the student's spec update email and fix-priority ranking in the meeting context. Do not ask them to re-argue playtest priority; treat that as decided. Instead, ask engineering-facing questions only where the student's own email or ranking leaves implementation gaps. Choose from topics such as player-facing behavior, edge cases, reset or persistence behavior, tuning ownership, acceptance criteria, and what is strict design intent versus implementation choice. Do not force every topic if it is already clear or irrelevant. React cooperatively but push for precision. End once the most important gaps are covered by summarizing what you will implement and one open risk if the student's answers were unclear.
design_review.prepReferenceContent:
Your spec update email:
{{response.spec_update}}

Your priority ranking:
{{response.playtest_fix_priority}}

Possible gap checks Priya may adapt:
- What behavior is required for your chosen fix to work?
- Which edge cases or retries could break that behavior?
- What should reset, persist, refresh, or remain tunable?
- What acceptance criteria would let QA verify the patch?
- Which details are design intent, and which can Priya choose while implementing?
Team need: keep the patch small, tunable, and QA-verifiable.

deskWorkDesign.workSurface: in-person engineering handoff with reference workspace

inputArtifacts.label: Engineering Handoff Workspace
inputArtifacts.summary: Email-driven implementation gaps, relevant edge cases, tuning rules, acceptance criteria, and implementation boundaries.

inputArtifacts.label: Your Prep Note (optional)
inputArtifacts.summary: The player's optional prep note from the previous scene, if provided.

deskWorkDesign.studentAction: Clarify buildable gameplay rules and answer engineering edge-case questions.
continuity: The engineering handoff transcript is the final communication artifact before assessment.
deskWorkDesign.authenticityCheck: A mid-level designer must clarify implementation rules live with engineering, not just write broad intent.

design_review.imageBrief: First-person view in a bright AAA game studio huddle room beside a playtest lab. Priya Raman, a practical gameplay engineer in her early 30s with tied-back dark hair, sits across the table with a laptop showing unreadable code-like blocks, a controller, and compact design notes nearby. Warm professional illustration, no real company logos.

assessment_gate.title: The Day Is Complete
assessment_gate.content: The simulated design day is complete. Your Wind Bud notes, playtest priority ranking, spec update email, and engineering handoff transcript are ready for assessment.
assessment_gate.imageBrief: A calm end-of-day game studio desk with a controller, notebook, and completed design workspace on a warm cream laptop. No assessment scores visible.

grading.title: Assessment
grading.imageBrief: A calm assessment moment represented by a warm game studio desk with the completed design workspace closed, a controller, and soft studio background. No scores, charts, grading text, or report cards visible.

final_report.title: Final Report
final_report.imageBrief: A polished final wrap-up scene at a game studio desk with a laptop showing an abstract report layout with unreadable blocks, completed notes, a controller, and concept art in the background. No exact scores or readable assessment text in the image.

playtest_workspace.title: Run the Playtest
playtest_workspace.content: You are in the playtest lab watching three first-time players run the Wind Bud room after your Teaching Note. Use the watchlist you wrote earlier, but do not assume your fix worked. Watch each moment, then record only behavior you can actually observe.
playtest_workspace.prompt: Create the observation log from the playtest. For each tester, write one conclusion with evidence: what you observed, whether it proves success/confusion/noise, and the design implication you would carry into your priority ranking.
playtest_workspace.priorityPrompt: Rank the fixes you wrote in the tester notes, from highest priority to lowest priority, and justify the order.
playtest_workspace.emptyWatchlistText: Submit the Watchlist first to see it here.

events.headline: Pauses on the safe start
events.description: Tester A stops on the safe starting platform instead of moving toward the Wind Bud. The pause is useful because it shows where their first read begins.

events.headline: Looks toward the optional coins
events.description: Tester A turns attention toward the optional coin trail before engaging the core Wind Bud rule. This may be a reward-placement distraction, not proof that the bud cue worked.

events.headline: Ignores the Wind Bud until the tutorial hint
events.description: The tutorial hint appears before Tester A notices the bud. That means the readable cue may still depend on instruction instead of player discovery.

events.headline: Strikes the Wind Bud late
events.description: Tester A eventually strikes the bud, but only after the hint. Log this as late comprehension, not a clean first-read success.

events.headline: Notices the Wind Bud quickly
events.description: Tester B turns toward the Wind Bud almost immediately. This is a positive recognition signal, but it does not yet prove the movement rule is readable.

events.headline: Strikes the Wind Bud quickly
events.description: Tester B strikes the bud without waiting for the tutorial hint. This supports the object cue, but the next moments decide whether the gust behavior is clear.

events.headline: Runs away from the gust
events.description: When the gust appears behind them, Tester B runs away from it. The object was readable, but the gust direction or safe-side entry is not.

events.headline: Falls before recovering
events.description: Tester B falls after misreading the gust entry. This is higher-impact than a preference note because it blocks progression through the first teach.

events.headline: Retries after the fall
events.description: Tester B resets and tries again. Recovery is possible, but the first attempt still shows the teaching beat did not fully explain how to use the gust.

events.headline: Turns toward the bud
events.description: Tester C notices the Wind Bud and turns toward it from the safe start. This is the cleanest first-read recognition in the session.

events.headline: Uses the gust from the safe side
events.description: Tester C enters the gust from the intended safe side. This is evidence that the core rule can work for some players.

events.headline: Reaches the landing
events.description: Tester C lands on the next platform. This is a success signal for the room's core progression path.

events.headline: Asks whether the reward ledge is optional
events.description: After success, Tester C asks about the reward ledge. Because the core rule already worked, this is likely backlog or reward clarity, not the main priority driver.

observationItems.prompt: Summarize the safe-start pause, coin attention, tutorial-hint dependency, and late bud strike.
Summarize the quick bud recognition, wrong-side gust read, fall, and retry.
Summarize the successful core path and decide how much weight to give the reward ledge question.

deskWorkDesign.workSurface: visual playtest timeline with observation log

inputArtifacts.label: Wind Bud Playtest Watchlist
inputArtifacts.summary: The player's observable success/failure/noise criteria from the prior scene.

inputArtifacts.label: Visual tester run timeline
inputArtifacts.summary: Moment-by-moment observed behavior from Tester A, Tester B, and Tester C.

deskWorkDesign.studentAction: Record observed tester behavior, separate success, confusion, and noise, then rank fix priorities with justification.

outputArtifact.label: Run the Playtest observation log
outputArtifact.format: one conclusion-with-evidence note per tester plus fix-priority ranking

continuity: The observation log and fix-priority ranking feed the spec update and engineering handoff.
deskWorkDesign.authenticityCheck: The player experiences the playtest loop by comparing their watchlist against actual tester behavior instead of re-reading a solved problem.

playtest_workspace.imageBrief: First-person view in a game studio playtest lab. A tester sits at a console station playing a whimsical forest platforming room while the designer watches from a laptop with an observation log, controller, stopwatch, and notebook nearby. Keep all screen text generic or unreadable; the simulator renders the readable observation notes.

maya.name: Maya Chen
maya.persona: Maya is calm, precise, and deeply player-focused. She likes playful ideas, but she pushes designers to prove how a player will understand the mechanic without the designer in the room. She is warm when the student owns tradeoffs and direct when a proposal hides scope risk.
maya.voice: Measured, encouraging, and specific. She asks one pointed follow-up before making a decision.

leo.name: Leo Navarro
leo.persona: Leo is playful, quick, and visually oriented. He cares about the player's first look, the rhythm of a room, and whether a blockout teaches through movement rather than explanation. He loves charming details but respects a clean first teach.
leo.voice: Energetic, sketchbook-in-hand, occasionally joking but never vague.

priya.name: Priya Raman
priya.persona: Priya is practical, kind, and constraint-aware. She wants the smallest clear implementation that can be tuned and tested. She appreciates designers who separate desired player experience from exact engineering implementation.

emi.name: Emi Tanaka
emi.persona: Emi is organized, warm, and quietly firm about milestone scope. She is not anti-creativity; she just wants the team to make the right call for the next playable build.

owen.name: Owen Brooks
owen.persona: Owen is direct, observant, and evidence-minded. He trusts designers who read the notes carefully and turn them into testable criteria. He dislikes vague handoffs that make QA guess what changed.
owen.voice: Dry, concrete, and focused on what can be verified.

intro.welcomeBody: Step into a mid-level Game Designer role at Starling Grove Studios. You will turn playtest confusion into a readable, testable design change for a fictional AAA console adventure.

steps.label: Read the build
steps.content: Start with the feature brief, Slack context, and playtest signals before designing.

steps.label: Make the work
steps.content: Create a teaching note, a playtest watchlist, a fix-priority ranking, a spec update, and a handoff.

steps.label: Align the team
steps.content: Clarify implementation rules in an in-person engineering handoff and close the loop with a clear email.

glossary.blockout: A rough, ugly playable version of a level used to test layout and flow before art polish.
glossary.hook: The first clear reason a player wants to try a mechanic.
glossary.affordance: A visual or audio cue that suggests how an object can be used.
glossary.fail-safe: A design choice that lets players recover after a mistake without frustration.
glossary.acceptance criteria: Specific conditions that tell the team whether a design change is implemented correctly.
glossary.telemetry: Data from playtests or builds that shows what players did.
glossary.milestone: A scheduled development checkpoint with agreed deliverables.
glossary.priority ranking: Ordering fixes by urgency, player impact, evidence strength, and feasibility.

wind_bud_teaching_note.attention_effect: Add gentle Wind Bud motion while the rest of the flowers stay still, with leaves drifting toward the gap.
wind_bud_teaching_note.success_feedback: Add a bud-opening animation, gust reveal, and short sound cue so the player knows this object caused the wind.

wind_bud_playtest_watchlist.observed_behavior: Watch whether the player turns toward the Wind Bud and approaches it before the tutorial hint.
wind_bud_playtest_watchlist.success_signal: The player strikes the bud, enters the gust from the safe side, and reaches the next platform without verbal help.
wind_bud_playtest_watchlist.failure_signal: The player walks past the bud, treats it as background art, or enters the gust from the wrong side.
wind_bud_playtest_watchlist.ignore_noise: Do not count a player saying the bud is cute as proof unless their movement shows they understood what it does.

playtest_observation_log.conclusion_with_evidence: Confusion signal: Tester A paused on the safe start, looked toward the coins, and only noticed the Wind Bud after the tutorial hint. In the priority ranking, treat the first-read cue as not solved; do not count the late strike as clean success.

Mixed signal: Tester B recognized and struck the bud quickly, but ran away when the gust appeared behind them and fell before retrying. In the priority ranking, put gust orientation or safe-side entry feedback before reward polish.

Success for the core rule: Tester C turned toward the bud, used the gust from the safe side, and reached the landing. The reward ledge question came after success, so keep it as backlog/noise unless it affects first-use comprehension.

sample_answers.playtest_fix_priority: A strong priority ranking puts the observed gust-read/entry problem first because Tester B recognized the bud but still failed the core movement rule, then names first-read cue or coin-pull concerns above lower-impact reward ledge feedback.
sample_answers.spec_update: A strong spec update names the observed player confusion, describes the cue or feedback change, gives expected behavior, defines acceptance criteria, and notes QA checks.
sample_answers.design_review_prep: If provided, a strong prep note states the intended player-facing behavior, names at least one edge case, defines reset or persistence behavior, and separates design intent from implementation choice.

criteria.name: 仕組みの問題整理
criteria.rubric_text: 9-10: 遊びの仕組み、プレイヤーがつまずく理由、学ばせたい体験、改善仮説を具体的に整理できている。この点数は、指定されたそのタスクだけを根拠にし、前後の別タスク、準備メモ、後続成果物は根拠にしない。 7-8: 主要要素は満たすが、根拠、具体性、優先順位、記録、または相手への応答に小さな抜けがある。4-6: 一部はできているが、重要な判断、確認、説明、記録、または次の行動に複数の抜けがある。0-3: タスクの目的を外す、根拠を無視する、危険な約束や混乱を生む、または提出がほぼ使えない。

criteria.name: 試遊観察リスト
criteria.rubric_text: 9-10: 試遊で観察する行動、成功条件、混乱サイン、聞くべき確認を事前に明確化できている。この点数は、指定されたそのタスクだけを根拠にし、前後の別タスク、準備メモ、後続成果物は根拠にしない。 7-8: 主要要素は満たすが、根拠、具体性、優先順位、記録、または相手への応答に小さな抜けがある。4-6: 一部はできているが、重要な判断、確認、説明、記録、または次の行動に複数の抜けがある。0-3: タスクの目的を外す、根拠を無視する、危険な約束や混乱を生む、または提出がほぼ使えない。

criteria.name: 試遊実行
criteria.rubric_text: 9-10: 試遊中の観察事実と解釈を分け、プレイヤーの学習、失敗、楽しさの変化を記録できている。この点数は、指定されたそのタスクだけを根拠にし、前後の別タスク、準備メモ、後続成果物は根拠にしない。 7-8: 主要要素は満たすが、根拠、具体性、優先順位、記録、または相手への応答に小さな抜けがある。4-6: 一部はできているが、重要な判断、確認、説明、記録、または次の行動に複数の抜けがある。0-3: タスクの目的を外す、根拠を無視する、危険な約束や混乱を生む、または提出がほぼ使えない。

criteria.name: 仕様更新
criteria.rubric_text: 9-10: 試遊で得た根拠をもとに、仕様変更、残す挙動、開発上の注意、未確認事項を明確に書けている。この点数は、指定されたそのタスクだけを根拠にし、前後の別タスク、準備メモ、後続成果物は根拠にしない。 7-8: 主要要素は満たすが、根拠、具体性、優先順位、記録、または相手への応答に小さな抜けがある。4-6: 一部はできているが、重要な判断、確認、説明、記録、または次の行動に複数の抜けがある。0-3: タスクの目的を外す、根拠を無視する、危険な約束や混乱を生む、または提出がほぼ使えない。

criteria.name: 設計説明通話
criteria.rubric_text: 9-10: 準備メモではなく実際の通話で、設計意図、仕様変更の理由、実装上の制約、未決事項を開発者へ説明できている。この点数は、指定されたそのタスクだけを根拠にし、前後の別タスク、準備メモ、後続成果物は根拠にしない。 7-8: 主要要素は満たすが、根拠、具体性、優先順位、記録、または相手への応答に小さな抜けがある。4-6: 一部はできているが、重要な判断、確認、説明、記録、または次の行動に複数の抜けがある。0-3: タスクの目的を外す、根拠を無視する、危険な約束や混乱を生む、または提出がほぼ使えない。
